whenToUse: Cross-field validation needed before object creation
Object should be validated and complete before use
Construction has a specific sequence or rules
Same construction process needs to create different representations
You need computed/derived fields calculated from other fields
whenNotToUse: Object is intentionally mutable (setters are fine)
Creation is trivial -- just new X(a, b)
Kotlin/Python named arguments solve your readability problem

structure.Product: The complex object with private constructor -- identity fields final, lifecycle fields can be mutable (Meal)
structure.Builder: Static inner class with fluent setters returning 'this', and build() with validation (Meal.Builder)
structure.Client: Creates Builder, configures via fluent API, calls build()

cheatSheet.sixWordSummary: Stage it. Validate it. Build it.
cheatSheet.codeSmellTrigger: Constructor with 5+ parameters, half of them null or false

bestPractices: Identity fields final (orderId, amount). Lifecycle fields (status, timestamps) can be mutable with controlled access.
Product constructor private -- only Builder can create it
build() validates ALL constraints -- required fields, cross-field rules, ranges
Return 'this' from every setter -- enables fluent chaining
Defensive copy collections in Product constructor -- List.copyOf(), not direct reference
Consider Optional<T> for nullable getters in Java -- useful but not mandatory
Separate Builder per Product type -- never a God Builder
Extract repeated build sequences into Director methods

commonMistakes.mistake: Product has setters on identity fields (orderId, amount), allowing mutation of what Builder validated
commonMistakes.correct: Identity fields final, no setters. Lifecycle fields (status) can have setters. Builder validates at birth.

commonMistakes.mistake: build() just copies fields with no validation
commonMistakes.correct: build() must validate required fields, cross-field constraints, and compute derived values.

commonMistakes.mistake: Sharing mutable collection references between Builder and Product
commonMistakes.correct: Defensive copy: this.items = List.copyOf(builder.items); in Product constructor.
Use plain constructor for simple objects. Builder earns its place with 4+ fields, optionals, or validation.

commonMistakes.mistake: Passing Builder around to multiple methods, losing track of state
commonMistakes.correct: Create -> configure -> build in one fluent chain. Don't let builders wander.

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing Builder (HOW to configure complex object) with Factory (WHICH type to create)
commonMistakes.correct: Factory picks the type in one call. Builder configures step-by-step. Combine when you need both.
